channel.title: SOC Intelligence Digest
channel.description: Seven-day operational threat intelligence and IOC reporting for SOC review. Coverage window: 2026/06/29 23:11 - 2026/07/06 23:11 JST. Article dates: 2026/06/29 - 2026/07/06 JST.

item.title: Mid-Year Threat Trends: What H1 2026 Signals for the Rest of the Year
item.description: Source: Cyble / Source attributes: Singapore / Vendor / Primary / Categories: Threat Intel, Vulnerability, Exploit, Malware, Ransomware, APT, Supply Chain, Identity, Data Breach, AI Security, Network, Incident Response, Threat Actor / Topics: Fortinet, Ransomware, Supply Chain, Vulnerability, Exploit, Malware, APT, Identity, Data Breach, AI Security / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: APT / targeted campaign (rank 6) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: APT / targeted campaign, EN attack activity, EN detection/analysis, EN targeted/APT/state-backed, EN critical sector, APT/campaign priority, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, indicator context, critical sector, primary references 1 / Summary: The first half of 2026 has given security teams little room to breathe. Ransomware operators kept up a punishing pace. If that wasn’t enough, access brokers turned network intrusions into a marketplace, and nation-state activity blurred further into hacktivism and organized cybercrime. Taken together, the numbers point to a threat landscape that isn't just growing louder; it's becoming faster, more coordinated, and harder to attribute. Cyble's monthly and quarterly research has tracked this shift in real time, and the pattern across regions is consistent. In short, attackers are scaling operat / Primary source: Cyble https://cyble.com/blog/2026-threat-intelligence-trends/ / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: Indirect Prompt Injection in Web Content Targets AI Agents
item.description: Source: Zscaler / Source attributes: United States / Research / Primary / Categories: Threat Intel, Phishing, Threat Actor, Supply Chain, AI Security, Cryptocurrency, Application Security / Topics: Supply Chain, Phishing, AI Security / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN detection/analysis, campaign priority, IOC 14, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, primary references 1 / Summary: IntroductionAI agents are increasingly changing how users interact with web content, making the content itself a growing attack surface for threat actors. Just as a human user can be socially engineered through phishing, AI agents are also susceptible to similar attacks. Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an example of these types of attacks that embed malicious instructions in the content retrieved by an AI agent (websites, documents, email, etc.) to influence the agent’s reasoning during task execution. Zscaler ThreatLabz has observed malicious websites that impersonate legitimate services a / Primary source: Zscaler https://www.zscaler.com/blogs/security-research/indirect-prompt-injection-web-content-targets-ai-agents / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: Google’s Continued Disruption of Malicious Residential Proxy Networks
item.description: Source: Google Cloud Threat Intelligence / Source attributes: United States / Research / Primary / Categories: Threat Intel, Vulnerability, Malware, APT, Cloud, DDoS, Mobile, Network, Threat Actor / Topics: Android, Vulnerability, Malware, APT, Cloud / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: APT / targeted campaign (rank 6) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: APT / targeted campaign, EN attack activity, EN IOC/detection/hunting, EN detection/analysis, EN targeted/APT/state-backed, JA attack activity, APT/campaign priority, IOC 80, linked IOC evidence, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, indicator context, primary references 1 / Summary: Background Today, in coordination with the FBI, Lumen, and others, Google took action against the NetNut residential proxy network, also known as Popa. This action builds on our disruption of the IPIDEA proxy network that took place in January 2026, and is a continuation of Google’s objective to dismantle malicious residential proxy networks. Actions Taken As a part of this disruption we took the following actions: Disabled Google accounts and associated Google services used by NetNut for malware command and control (C2), which directly violates Google’s Terms of Service and Acceptable Use Pol / Primary source: Google Cloud Threat Intelligence https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/google-continued-disruption-residential-proxy-networks/ / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: New ChocoPoC RAT Targets Vulnerability Researchers via Fake PoC Exploit Repos
item.description: Source: The HackersNews / Source attributes: United States / News / Secondary / Categories: Vulnerability, Exploit, Malware / Topics: Vulnerability, Exploit, Malware / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (89) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN attack activity, EN active exploit/PoC/zero-day, JA active exploit/PoC/zero-day, campaign priority, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, compound vulnerability exploit impact detection context, primary references 3 / Summary: Attackers are hiding a data-stealing trojan inside fake exploit code aimed at the people who hunt bugs for a living. The malware, called ChocoPoC, travels in Python proof-of-concept (PoC) repositories on GitHub that claim to exploit hot new CVEs. Run one, and it quietly lifts your saved passwords, browser cookies, and files, then hands the attacker a shell on your machine. YesWeHack and / Primary source: Trend Micro https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/25/a/information-stealer-masquerades-as-ldapnightmare-poc-exploit.html

item.title: Japan’s Aflac, KDDI, Sapporo, Nidec: Four Breaches, One Common Entry Point
item.description: Source: The Cyber Express / Source attributes: Global / News / Secondary / Categories: Threat Intel, Vulnerability, Ransomware, Threat Actor, Data Breach, Network / Topics: Network Edge, Ransomware, Vulnerability, Data Breach / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (77) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN attack activity, EN detection/analysis, EN Japan/Asia relevance, EN critical sector, campaign priority, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, indicator context, Japan related, critical sector, primary references 1 / Summary: Four major Japan cyberattacks reported within two weeks point to a common trend, with attackers gaining access through subsidiaries and third-party infrastructure rather than corporate headquarters. While the incidents affected companies from different industries, including insurance, telecommunications, brewing, and manufacturing, the breaches shared one notable characteristic. Rather than directly compromising corporate headquarters, attackers gained access through subsidiaries, overseas operations, or third-party infrastructure. The affected organizations include Aflac Japan, KDDI, Sapporo / Primary source: Cyble https://cyble.com/knowledge-hub/what-is-social-engineering/

item.title: Alleged Scattered Spider Member Arrested in Finland, Extradited to U.S.
item.description: Source: The Cyber Express / Source attributes: Global / News / Secondary / Categories: Threat Intel, Patch, Ransomware, Phishing, Threat Actor, Data Breach, Network, Incident Response, Cryptocurrency, APT / Topics: Network Edge, Ransomware, VMware, Phishing, Data Breach, APT / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (73) / Focus: APT / targeted campaign (rank 6) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: APT / targeted campaign, EN attack activity, EN critical sector, APT/campaign priority, coherent attack event, target and impact context, indicator context, uncertain reporting, critical sector, primary references 2 / Summary: An alleged member of the Scattered Spider cybercrime group has been extradited from Finland to the United States to face federal charges related to conspiracy, cyber intrusion, and fraud. U.S. authorities said the case marks another step in their ongoing efforts to prosecute individuals accused of participating in high-profile cybercrime operations linked to the notorious hacking group. Peter Stokes, 19, a dual U.S. and Estonian citizen, made his initial appearance in federal court in Chicago after being extradited from Finland. According to the U.S. Department of Justice , Stokes was arrested / Primary source: justice.gov https://www.justice.gov/usao-ndil/pr/alleged-member-criminal-cyber-hacking-group-scattered-spider-arrested-finland-and

item.title: The Platform You Trust Is the Platform They Target
item.description: Source: Cofense / Source attributes: United States / Vendor / Primary / Categories: Threat Intel, Malware, Phishing, Threat Actor, Identity, Mobile, Endpoint / Topics: Android, Apple/iOS, Microsoft, Malware, Phishing, Identity / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN attack activity, campaign priority, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, primary references 1 / Summary: Cofense Intelligence is observing a clear shift in phishing operations: threat actors are moving beyond broad, one-size-fits-all campaigns and adopting platform - aware delivery that adapts to the victim’s device, browser, and environment. What began as simple Windows - focused malware distribution campaigns has evolved into more sophisticated campaigns that can selectively deliver credential phishing, remote access tools, or malware across Windows, MacOS, and Android. This trend reflects a broader strategic change in the threat landscape, one that is designed to increase the likelihood of com / Primary source: Cofense https://cofense.com/blog/the-platform-you-trust-is-the-platform-they-target / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: Doctor Web’s Q2 2026 review of virus activity on mobile devices
item.description: Source: Dr.Web / Source attributes: Russia / Vendor / Primary / Categories: Malware, Phishing, Threat Actor, Mobile, Endpoint, Detection / Topics: Android, Microsoft, Malware, Phishing / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN attack activity, EN detection/analysis, EN critical sector, campaign priority, IOC 57, linked IOC evidence, target and impact context, detection evidence context, critical sector, primary references 1 / Summary: July 1, 2026 According to detection statistics collected by Dr.Web Security Space for mobile devices, Android.Banker banking trojans were less active in the second quarter (Q2) of 2026—by 31.83%, compared to the first quarter (Q1). At the same time, they remained the most widespread Android malware: this family accounted for 23.28% of all malware detections. These trojans steal various confidential data, such as SMS containing one-time codes from credit organizations and logins and passwords for accessing online bank accounts, and they can also display phishing windows. As in the previous quar / Primary source: Dr.Web https://news.drweb.com/show/?i=15274&lng=en&c=5 / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: Doctor Web’s Q2 2026 virus activity review
item.description: Source: Dr.Web / Source attributes: Russia / Vendor / Primary / Categories: Vulnerability, Exploit, Malware, Ransomware, Phishing, Threat Actor, Mobile, Endpoint, Detection, Cryptocurrency / Topics: Android, Apple/iOS, Microsoft, Ransomware, Vulnerability, Exploit, Malware, Phishing / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN attack activity, EN detection/analysis, EN critical sector, campaign priority, IOC 3, linked IOC evidence, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, critical sector, primary references 1 / Summary: July 1, 2026 According to statistics collected by the Dr.Web anti-virus, the total number of threats detected in the second quarter (Q2) of 2026 was up by 5.72% from Q1. The number of unique threats increased by 95.32%. Ad-displaying programs and trojans, malicious crypto-miners, and trojans used to run other malware were most frequently discovered on protected devices. In email traffic, malicious scripts and different types of trojans, such as downloaders, droppers, password stealers, and miners, were most regularly detected. Also commonly encountered were backdoors, phishing documents, and v / Primary source: Dr.Web https://news.drweb.com/show/?i=15275&lng=en&c=5 / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: Glitch SPY: An Emerging Android RAT Distributed Through a Fake Polish Rental App
item.description: Source: Cyble / Source attributes: Singapore / Vendor / Primary / Categories: Malware, Threat Intel, Threat Actor, Supply Chain, Identity, Mobile, Cryptocurrency / Topics: Android, Supply Chain, Malware, Identity / Priority: Critical (100) / SOC: 100 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN attack activity, EN detection/analysis, campaign priority, IOC 3, coherent attack event, target and impact context, detection evidence context, indicator context, primary references 1 / Summary: Executive Summary Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs identified an emerging Android malware family tracked as Glitch SPY , distributed through a fraudulent Polish apartment and house rental platform designed to lure users into downloading an Android APK. Based on the Polish-language lure and rental-themed distribution website, the activity appears to be Poland-focused, targeting users in Poland or Polish expats. The downloaded application functions as a dropper and installs the Glitch SPY payload after convincing the user to allow installation from unknown sources. Glitch SPY prompts the vic / Primary source: Cyble https://cyble.com/blog/glitch-spy-rat-distributed-via-fake-polish-app/ / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: GPT-5.5-Cyber built a zlib fuzzing lab in a day
item.description: Source: Trail of Bits / Source attributes: Global / Research / Primary / Categories: Research, Application Security, Vulnerability, Exploit, Patch, AI Security / Topics: Vulnerability, Exploit, AI Security / Priority: Critical (91) / SOC: 62 / Confidence: High (100) / Focus: attack campaign / intrusion (rank 5) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: attack campaign / intrusion, EN active exploit/PoC/zero-day, campaign priority, coherent attack event, detection evidence context, primary references 1 / Summary: We’re running Patch the Planet , an ongoing collaboration with OpenAI that pairs Trail of Bits engineers directly with more than 30 open-source projects. Its goal is to front-run a serious problem facing open-source maintainers: highly capable models like GPT-5.5-Cyber will soon create a firehose of bug reports, and OSS maintainers are already spread thin. Our plan is to point OpenAI’s latest models at real codebases, find the security bugs first, work with maintainers to patch them, and find ways to decrease the burden on maintainers in the long run. We’ll publish field reports like this one / Primary source: Trail of Bits https://blog.trailofbits.com/2026/07/02/field-reports-from-patch-the-planet/ / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: 絶対に止められない医療システムをどう守るか─医療情報システムの安全管理に関するガイドライン第7.0版を読み解く
item.description: Source: Cybereason / Source attributes: United States / Vendor / Primary / Categories: Ransomware, Endpoint, Incident Response / Topics: Ransomware / Priority: Critical (83) / SOC: 64 / Confidence: High (93) / Focus: incident / breach (rank 3) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: incident / breach, JA attack activity, JA Japan/Asia relevance, JA critical sector, Japan related, critical sector, primary references 1 / Summary: 診療を止めない。患者の情報を守る。これは医療に関わる誰もが共有する大前提であり、サイバーセキュリティが医療施設にとって不可欠である理由もここにあります。2026年6月、厚生労働省から正式に公表された「医療情報システムの安全管理に関するガイドライン第7.0版」は、この大前提を制度として明確にガイドライン化したものです。 2026年に入ってから、残念ながら国内医療業界のサイバーインシデントが続いています。ランサムウェアによる被害を中心に、ナースコールや電子カルテシステムの停止、それに伴う医療行為の停止を余儀なくされたケースもあります。医療業界のインシデント事例に関しては別の記事で記載していますので […続きを読む] / Primary source: Cybereason https://www.cybereason.co.jp/blog/edr/14282/ / Primary source age: 0 days

item.title: 国内ISPの認証情報を不正利用したフィッシングメールの注意喚起
item.description: Source: Canon MJ ESET Security / Source attributes: Japan / Research / Primary / Categories: Threat Intel, Malware, Phishing, Mobile / Topics: Malware, Phishing / Priority: High (67) / SOC: 55 / Confidence: High (81) / Focus: incident / breach (rank 3) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: incident / breach, JA attack activity, JA detection/analysis, JA Japan/Asia relevance, uncertain reporting, Japan related, primary references 1 / Summary: 2026年6月26日現在、国内ISPの認証情報（ID、メールアドレス、パスワード等）を不正利用（乗っ取り）して送信されたとみられるフィッシングが確認されております。 このようなフィッシングサイトにて、情報（メールアドレス、電話番号等）を入力しないよう、ご注意ください。 ご利用のISPやメールサービスのホームページ上の注意喚起やニュース等を確認し、漏えい対象のサービスをお使いの場合は、メールアカウントが乗っ取られる可能性があるため、速やかにサービスのパスワード変更等を行ってください。 もしご利用のサービスからメール等で連絡が来た場合は、メール等のリンクからではなく、すでにご利用のアプリやブラウザーのブックマークからサービスへアクセスして設定するなど、便乗したフィッシングにご注意ください。 【メールの件名】 【重要】VISA力一ド ご利用制限のお知らせ 【重要】お客様のAmazonアカウントが一時的に制限されています 【重要】アカウントが一時的に制限されています 【Amazon.co.jp】お支払い方法の確認が必要です — アカウント利用制限中 【Amazon.co.jp】お荷物の配送に問題が発生しました お荷物の配送状況に関するご連絡 お荷物の配送に問題が発生しました 荷物のお届けに問題がございます お届け予定の荷物に問題が見つかりました お届け中の荷物に異常が確認されました ※ 上 / Primary source: Canon MJ ESET Security https://eset-info.canon-its.jp/malware_info/news/detail/260630.html / Primary source age: 0 days

The activity has been found to weaponize CVE-2026-33017 (CVSS score: 9.3), an unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Langflow, indicating threat actors are scanning and targeting exposed artificial intelligence (AI) / Primary source: Trend Micro https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/26/f/from-langflow-to-monero-inside-cve-2026-33017-cryptominer.html

item.title: U.S. Government Entity Paid Kairos $1 Million in Data-Theft Extortion Case
item.description: Source: The HackersNews / Source attributes: United States / News / Secondary / Categories: Ransomware / Topics: Ransomware / Priority: Medium (56) / SOC: 52 / Confidence: Medium (58) / Focus: incident / breach (rank 3) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: incident / breach, EN attack activity, EN critical sector, target and impact context, critical sector, primary references 1 / Summary: A U.S. government entity paid about $1 million to keep stolen files from being leaked, according to a new case study by Rakesh Krishnan for Ransom-ISAC, built on a leaked negotiation chat and the blockchain trail the payment left. The odd part: the group that took the money calls itself Kairos, but it may not be a ransomware gang at all. Krishnan found no sign that it ever locked a single / Primary source: ransom-isac.org https://ransom-isac.org/blog/kairos-ransomware-data-extortion-case-study/

item.title: FBI Seizes NetNut Proxy Platform, Popa Botnet
item.description: Source: Krebs on Security / Source attributes: United States / News / Secondary / Categories: Threat Intel, Malware / Topics: Malware / Priority: Medium (55) / SOC: 55 / Confidence: High (86) / Focus: IOC / detection evidence (rank 2) / Reference count: 1 / Reasons: focus: IOC / detection evidence, EN attack activity, IOC 12, linked IOC evidence, target and impact context, primary references 3, IOC/detection-only priority cap / Summary: The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) said today it worked with industry partners to seize hundreds of domains associated with NetNut, a sprawling residential proxy service operated by the publicly-traded Israeli company Alarum Technologies [NASDAQ: ALAR]. The action comes roughly two weeks after KrebsOnSecurity published findings from multiple security firms connecting NetNut to the Popa botnet, a collection of at least two million devices that have been compromised by malicious software with little or no consent from victims. / Primary source: spur.us https://spur.us/blog/smart-tv-apps-residential-proxy-sdks
